title: Ancient History
whatItIs:
Ancient history studies human societies before the modern world, from Mesopotamia and Egypt to Greece, Rome, early China, India, the Americas, and the first great empires of Eurasia. It is a field built from texts, artefacts, ruins, inscriptions, coins, architecture, and burial evidence.

Because the ancient world is so distant, the discipline depends on careful reconstruction. Historians and archaeologists piece together fragmentary evidence to understand politics, trade, religion, war, law, and daily life.

The ancient past is not one story. It is a collection of overlapping civilisations with different technologies, belief systems, and social orders that rose, adapted, and often collapsed.
whyItMatters:
Ancient history matters because many of the structures we still use were first invented there: writing, cities, law codes, coinage, bureaucracy, calendars, and empire.

It also matters because the ancient world gives perspective. It reminds us that modern life is not the first attempt at civilisation, and that power, fragility, and inequality are old human problems.

Studying ancient history also trains humility. We are forced to infer a great deal from incomplete evidence, which sharpens judgment and makes people less certain than they ought to be.
keyIdeas: Civilisation begins with writing, cities, and administrative complexity
Empires centralised power but also created scale and exchange
Religion and myth were not separate from politics or law
Trade networks connected distant regions long before modernity
Collapse and transition were common, not exceptional
Archaeology and texts must be read together
The ancient world was diverse, not a single civilisation
Ancient history trains inference from fragmentary evidence
frontier:
Ancient history is being transformed by ancient DNA, isotopic analysis, satellite archaeology, and better digital imaging of inscriptions and manuscripts. These tools reveal movement, diet, disease, and urban planning that older scholarship could only guess at.

There is also increasing attention to global ancient history rather than just Greek and Roman narratives. The field is broadening to include Africa, the Americas, South Asia, and Central Asia on equal footing.

The frontier is therefore about method and scope: better evidence, wider geography, and more plural stories of the ancient world.
openQuestions: Which ancient institutions were most durable, and why?
How much can we really know from fragmentary evidence?
What caused ancient civilisations to expand or collapse?
How should modern people interpret ancient empires morally?
Which ancient ideas still shape politics, law, and religion?
How do we compare civilisations without flattening their differences?
